text: No portions of this operation were edited out. It was played at four times the regular speed, however. The objective is to show that this approach does not add excessive operative time. This was performed in twenty-four minutes. This patient had one previous operation for a perforated appendicitis approximately six years ago. The ventral incisional hernia was from the umbilical trocar site. Omental adhesions to the anterior abdominal wall are easily removed. The hernia is estimated to be approximately two centimeters in diameter. A robotic approach may seem like overkill in this situation. However, the final repair is a superior result for many reasons.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap, revealing herniated preperitoneal adipose tissue. The full defect is exposed, and a 6.4 cm piece of mesh is prepared for placement. The preperitoneal pocket is extended circumferentially to accommodate the mesh, with the peritoneal lining separated from the posterior rectus fascia to ensure stronger incorporation.

The surgeon identifies the hernia sac as a small protrusion on the peritoneal layer, with its contents inverted back into the abdominal cavity. The assistant hands over the suture material and mesh, which is placed into the prepared pocket. The hernia defect is closed primarily using a running absorbable barbed suture, with stitches spaced approximately one centimeter apart to evenly distribute tension and reduce the risk of seroma formation.

The surgeon places all sutures around the hernia defect before tightening them, ensuring the defect remains visible during the process. The barbed suture is secured with additional throws to maintain tension, and the final pass incorporates the mesh fixation at the center of the hernia.
The surgeon circumferentially sutures the mesh to the posterior fascial wall using an absorbable barbed suture, ensuring the mesh remains in apposition during healing.

There are many benefits to this operative approach that are shown in this video. First, the herniated contents are completely reduced back into the abdominal space. Patients appreciate a better result when this is done. Second, the hernia defect is closed primarily, preventing the hernia from becoming larger over time and reducing the potential for seroma formation. Third, the mesh is fixated to the sturdy and robust rectus fascia, which prevents the mesh from migrating. Using sutures for this step saves the expense of the absorbable tacking device. Finally, the peritoneal space is completely closed over the mesh, concealing it from the intestine and preventing adhesions and potential bowel obstructions in the future.

A benefit that is not seen here is the location and the size of the incisions required for this operation. This is done through three separate eight millimeter incisions that are placed on the side of the abdominal wall, far away from the location of the mesh. Mesh infections can be a very serious complication. Placing the incisions far away from the mesh reduces the risk that a skin infection would result in a mesh infection.
The surgeon completes the procedure by removing all suture material from the abdomen, indicating the end of the operation.
